title: Wise Woman: Mary Midgley - Philosophy Talk
text:
Coming up,
>> people think it's obvious that nothing
except [music] physical things are real.
Well, just watch how they live for the
next day or so.
>> The life and thought [music] of Mary
Migley,
>> one of the UK's most prominent public
intellectuals.
>> The trouble with this sort of [music]
talk, what would one call scientism, it
distracts people from thinking about
their own lives in the direct way that
they can. And I think it takes [music]
the place which perhaps some sorts of
theology used to take. Mitchley was
[music] one of the first eco feminists.
She also wrote about science, religion,
and human nature. She is a philosopher
of complexity.
>> Our guest is [music] Clara Mcool,
co-author of metaphysical animals. How
four women brought philosophy back to
life.
>> Conflict, contradiction, [music]
complexity. You know, that's just our
lot. And that's the kind of reality that
we're trying to navigate.
>> We are much more complicated beings
[music] than this notion.
Mary Migley. Coming up on Philosophy
Talk.
>> Hi, I'm Ray Briggs.
>> And I'm Josh Landy with an urgent
message. In early April, we were
informed that our grant from the
National Endowment for the Humanities to
produce the Wise Women series had been
terminated by the current
administration. This decision also puts
in jeopardy our recent proposal to the
NEH to do two more seasons of this
important series. That funding is now
clearly off the table, leaving us in a
very precarious position. Our future is
on the line.
>> We recognize that everyone is facing
challenging times. But if Philosophy
Talk is something you value and want to
see continue, please consider a gift of
support right now at
philosophytalk.org/support.
>> Every contribution helps. But if you can
find a way to give $500 or more, we'll
thank you by name in the credits for the
show.
>> In the meantime, as always, thank you
for listening
>> and thank you for thinking.
Can science explain everything?
>> Is there such a thing as human nature?
>> Aren't we more than just the sum of our
genes?
>> Welcome to Philosophy Talk, the program
that questions everything. [music]
>> Accept your intelligence. I'm Ray
Briggs.
>> And I'm Josh Landy. We're coming to you
via the studios of KALW San Francisco
Bay [music] Area.
>> Continuing conversations that begin at
Philosophers Corner on the Stanford
campus where Josh teaches philosophy
>> and the University of Chicago where Ray
teaches philosophy. Today it's the next
episode in our wise women series
generously supported by a grant from the
National Endowment for the Humanities.
We're exploring the life and thought of
Mary Mitchley,
>> one of the Oxford Quartet along with
Elizabeth Anskam, Iris Murdoch, and
Philip Aoot.
>> Yeah, we got shows on all four of them
in our series. Mitchley was also a big
public figure in the UK.
>> Right. I remember her being on the radio
when I was a kid.
>> Yeah, she talked about the value of the
humanities, about environmentalism,
about science. She loved science, but
she was very critical of a thing called
scientism, right? The the idea that
science can explain everything.
>> Wait, Josh. She thought there were
things that science can't explain. I I
don't want to be dismissive, but that
sounds a little bit woo woo.
>> Oh, I don't think so. I mean, she wasn't
some kind of mystic. She just thought
scientists should stick to, you know,
science and and stay out of domains they
don't belong, like ethics or
metaphysics.
>> Metaphysics? You mean the study of how
the world really is? That's exactly what
science is supposed to tell us. Physics
breaks the world down to into its most
basic parts. You can't get much more
real than that.
>> No, no, no, no. Physics breaks the world
down into its smallest physical parts.
But it can't tell us how living things
work. And it certainly can't tell us how
our minds work.
>> Fine. Maybe physics can't tell us that.
But that's why we have other sciences.
You've got biology, psychology,
neurochemistry. Poke around long enough
and you'll find a scientific explanation
for everything.
>> That's a horrifying thought, Rey. And I
don't think Migley would buy it either.
I think she'd say it doesn't matter how
much data you compile. There's always
going to be bigger questions that
science just can't answer.
>> Yeah, like what?
>> Well, take one of Miji's favorite
examples. How should we think about
nonhuman creatures? I don't see how
that's a counter example to anything
I've been saying. Science tells us that,
hey, cheetahs need a lot of space to
roam, but sloths, they're perfectly
happy staying in one place. It gives us
a lot of information about animals and
about how we should treat them.
>> I think Mitchi would object to your
whole way of framing the question, how
we should treat them. It's not a matter
of how we should treat animals. We are
animals. Midi thinks that we human
beings have invented this speurious
category called animals as though
they're one big lump and we're over to
the other side being all special.
>> Well, aren't we special in some ways? I
mean, aren't we at least special in
thinking that we're special?
>> Not even that. That's actually one of
Michel's coolest points. She says that
when a bird is singing, it's really
saying, "Hurrah, harrah, it's me. It's
mine. I've got it. I am the greatest."
>> Okay, I love that. But I still don't
understand how that tells us anything
about the limits of science. It seems
like science tells us what birds are
thinking. And and science tells us that
human beings are animals, too. Mitchley
thought it tells us even more. She
thought science tells us that the earth
is alive and that we have to take care
of the environment.
>> That's true. She was a big fan of the
Gaia hypothesis. The the thought that
everything on this planet is is part of
one giant living system.
>> But I don't get it. If even the Gaia
hypothesis is part of science, what is
left that science can't explain?
>> That's a really good question, Rey. I
bet our guest is going to have things to
say about it. It's Claire Mcool from
Durham University, co-author of
Metaphysical Animals, a really great
book about the Oxford Quartet.
>> But first, we sent our roving
philosophical reporter Sarah Li Sterland
to investigate Mijley's ideas about
animals and the Gaia hypothesis. She
files this report. Philosophers often
think humans are at the pinnacle of life
on Earth. But Mary Migley was more
concerned about whether there be any
life left to debate about. She said we
should pay respect to Gia, not as some
new age goddess, but as shorthand for
the incredibly complex systems we depend
on, but that we treat like rubbish. In
2014, she told biologist Tom Wakeford,
>> "Our relation to the earth is as [music]
close as our relation to the people
around us on whom we most depend. We
naturally love it. It has made us what
we are. We are part of it. We're
grateful to it and we respect it.
>> The name is based on a myth, but the GIA
hypothesis has actually been studied a
lot by scientists. James Lovelock and
Lynn Margolus first published a paper on
it in the early 1970s. They observed
that ecosystems, the ocean and the
atmosphere, interact and they form a
self-regulating planet. The system
maintained conditions that were
conducive to life for 3.2 billion years.
But by the 1970s, this balance was being
disrupted.
Mary Mijley was born in 1919 in London.
She spent her early years in the
countryside of Middle Sex. She studied
philosophy at Oxford and later taught at
Newcastle. In 1950, she married another
philosopher, Jeff Mijley, and they had
three sons. [music]
It wasn't until the 80s though that Miji
started writing about the implications
of the Gaia hypothesis. It grew out of
her interest in zoology. As she told BBC
broadcaster Sue Lley in 2005, what
really made it all come [music] together
was that I got passionately interested
in animal behavior [music]
um in the accounts given by uh people
like Jane Goodall and Conrad Lorent
[music]
and others of how animals actually
behave. Reading about animal behavior
made her challenge the 17th century
philosophers who claimed our intellect
set us apart from other species. It led
her to establish her lifelong thesis
about our animal nature and its
implications in her first book beast and
man.
>> She thinks that we should start doing
moral philosophy by thinking about what
human nature [music] is like. What kind
of animal is a human?
>> Ellie Robson is an ethics [music]
lecturer at King's College in London.
She says, "Miji argued that humans are
not just thinking machines. We're shaped
by both our biology and the world around
us.
>> We're lots of different kinds of things.
[music] We're social, emotional,
rational, playful, imaginative. Loads of
these things we share with non-human
animals."
>> So, what does it really mean to live by
Miji's credo in the 21st century?
Philosophy in the Wild is a new project
that invites [music] the public to
explore this. Our idea is to improve
philosophy and [music] come up with
inspiring and hopeful visions of what it
could be [music] for humans and other
animals to live together in a better
way. That's Mara Daria Kojakaru, a
philosopher poet in London. She's the
project [music] director for Philosophy
in the Wild. The project's goal is to
convene local groups of philosophers,
scientists, artists, kids, and other
species of animals around the globe to
explore how they can live in [music]
caring communities.
In April 2025, for example, a writer and
a local artist took sixth graders to
Norway's remote Vega Islands. There,
they learned how humans and wild IDs
cooperate. As part of a 400year-old
tradition, islanders build shelters for
the ducks during nesting season to
protect them from predators. After the
ducklings leave, humans collect valuable
down from the nests. A duvet's worth can
sell for thousands of dollars. Through
evolution, a relation has evolved
between humans and other animals where
both gain from the relation. So it's not
exploitative.
Other communities from Brazil to
Bangalore will convene to discuss their
relationships with dolphins, bats,
wolves, seals, bison, and feral dogs.
Each animal human team is tasked with
composing a quote unquote multiecies
poem.
>> A poem that includes also visual or
scent elements.
>> Kaaru believes smell connects us to our
animal nature. So each site has kits
with tins and glycerin to extract
[music] local scents. It's a playful and
creative approach to language and human
animal relations.
>> Kids and the kids inside of us are key
participants.
>> I think the [music] best review I once
got from a workshop on multiecies poetry
for [music] children was better than the
Halloween party last week. What do you
want more? Mary Migley said philosophers
should provide tools to help [music]
people unclog muddled thinking. Poetry
envisions new possibilities. [music]
>> When people get stuck by being obsessed
with a single way of looking [music] at
things, stand back and see what the
neighbors of this are.
>> For Philosophy Talk, I'm Sarah Li
Sterland.
>> Thanks for that super interesting
report, Sarah. I'm Josh Landy. With me
is my fellow philosopher, Ray Briggs,
and today we're asking about the life
and thought of Mary Mitchley.
>> We're joined now by Claire Mcool. She's
professor of philosophy at Durham
University in the UK and co-author of
Metaphysical Animals: How Four Women
Brought Philosophy Back to Life. Claire,
welcome to Philosophy Talk.
>> Thank you so much.
>> So, Claire, you actually knew Migley and
you visited her in a nursing home
towards the end of her life. Can you
tell us about the kinds of conversations
you had with her?
Yeah, I used to visit her with my
friend, my good friend and co-author
Rachel Wisman, who you had on the show
uh a little while back and we used to go
to Mary and talk to her about her time
at Oxford during World War II. So she
was a student there and she used to sit
in this amazing gray puffy very
supportive chair because she was 95 when
we first started going to talk to her
and she'd just tell us about the figures
that that taught her and they were huge
names like um Argie Collingwood Hage
Price and she'd just lean over and take
a book off the bookshelf and just you
know talk about these historical figures
as if they were in the room and there
was biscuits also always biscuits.
[laughter]
Yeah, she sounds very charming and uh we
know that uh she managed to reach a wide
audience with her philosophy including
Josh when he was a kid listening to the
radio. Uh what was she most known for in
the public imagination?
>> Well, she's most quickly but maybe a bit
too easily associated with a critique of
Richard Dawkins's idea of the selfish
gene. As you probably know, Dawkins
published this book on the selfish gene
in 1976, I think it was. Um and that's a
biology book, but Miji is really really
worried that that um in fact is telling
us giving us a picture of the kinds of
beings that we are that we're we're
selfish. And she's writing this critique
whatever developing all these arguments
against Dawkins in the 1980s. So this is
a period when um there's a conservative
government. So, you know, Margaret
Thatcher is in government and she's just
worried that this idea is going to seep
into public consciousness and maybe
start influencing kind of behavioral
economics and so on
>> because the idea is that if we're
naturally selfish, then it's okay to be
selfish. Is that the thought?
>> Exactly. And but apart from that, she's
also worried about, you know, Dawkins is
a scientist and he's using this word
really carefully in the way that a
scientist does with precision and that
gives it a kind of authority and she's
worried about that. Um, and she's also
saying, "Wait, stop the whole notion of
selfishness. It only makes sense." Um,
when you look at us in the context of
our kind of sociality, the fact that
we're social creatures, we do things
together, you know, we share, we look
after each other in infancy and old age
and so on. And it's only when someone is
not doing that that we call them
selfish. So, it not only kind of
distorts our understanding of ourselves,
it just gets us completely wrong from
the start. So,
>> yeah. So this is very interesting to me
because like on the one hand like
Dawkins actually isn't claiming in that
book that we are selfish. He's claiming
that our genes are selfish. So like I
think that there's a bit of room to
criticize. On the other hand given the
way that UK society has gone and kind of
Dawkins has gone like do you think that
she had a point about the rhetoric of
selfishness?
>> Yeah, it's very much about the rhetoric.
So one of Miji's big sort of claims is
that we're these kind of creatures who
create images and world myths and
narratives and we're we're those kinds
of creatures and we are guided and we
orientate ourselves in terms of these
myths and so if a myth at a particular
historical moment is harmful that's a
problem. So the idea of the selfish gene
in itself might have been okay at
another historical moment like so if
Dawkins was a Hobbes type figure and he
was trying to kind of overthrow feudal
structures or something it might have
been good in that historical context but
in the 1980s in Britain she thought this
is actually a really potentially
disastrous idea and that's what she was
objecting to and it it sort of ties into
what Josh was saying um at the start
about scientism.
>> Say more like what's what's the
connection there? Well, well, it's again
this idea that Dawkins is a scientist
and he's giving us this way of picturing
ourselves at a very rudimentary sort of
basic level um that is simple like he's
saying our primary motivation is you
know to compete and to win and that kind
of simplicity it's really easy to you
know start thinking you can apply a
picture like that just really widely.
Um, so it's the combination of the
apparent scientific precision with the
simplicity which is of course a virtue
in science that's a bit of a slippery
slope and you might start thinking that
this this very idea then just can start
infiltrating other explanatory domains
and start doing kind of work there where
it's really not needed and it's really
not at home.
>> You're listening to Philosophy Talk.
Today we're discussing the life and
thought of Mary Midgley with Clare
McCall from Durham University.
>> Is it okay to keep animals in zoos?
Should we all be vegetarians? Can
science answer these questions?
>> Human rights, animal rights, and rights
for the whole planet along with your
comments and questions when philosophy
talk continues.
[music]
and flies. I am [music]
[music]
>> Are we human beings as special as we
like to think we are or are we all just
ape men? I'm Josh Landy and this is
Philosophy Talk, the program that
questions everything.
>> Accept your intelligence. I'm Ray Briggs
and we're exploring the life and thought
of Mary Mitchley. Our guest is Claire
Mcool from Durham University, co-author
of Metaphysical Animals.
>> It's the latest episode in our WiseWomen
series, generously supported by a grant
from the National Endowment for the
Humanities. You can listen to all the
episodes in the series at our website,
philosophytalk.org/wisewomen.
And while you're there, you can also
become a subscriber and go wild in our
library of more than 600 episodes.
>> So Claire Mitchley wrote about a wide
range of topics, science, ethics, animal
rights. Is there one theme that unites
all of her ideas?
>> There is and it's actually the first
line of her first published book which
she wrote when she was well was
published when she was 57. So the book
is called beast and man and the first
line famous first line is we are not
just like animals we are animals and she
says herself like that is the starting
point from which my all of my philosophy
springs. Um so yeah so that is the line
the continuous thread that goes through
everything and there's 18 18 or 19 books
depending on how you count.
>> So what follows from that?
>> Well [laughter]
a lot follows from that. Okay. So you
can start with the you know the very
beginning which is she is not a
cartisian philosopher. So she's not a
philosopher who thinks that there's a
distinction between mind and matter. And
you know if you think there's that
distinction there's a real problem about
how they fit together again. But she
says, "No, we're actually animals. You
need to kind of go back before Daycart
to Aristotle and to the ancients. We're
animals. We're we're living things." So
that that's the start, but of course
it's only the start. I mean, she does so
much with that. So she she once said to
me, and I and I love this image. She
said that for her
um her Neil Armstrong was Jane Goodall,
the primatologist. I think when she said
that the thought there is that like what
the ethologist did does or what the you
know what Jane Goodall did was as
transformative to our self-
understanding as seeing the earth from
space
you know so the work of the ethologist
is a really kind of crucial feature of
her work and her method in that book in
beast and man is to draw on the work of
the ethologist to do moral philosophy
and that's I mean it's amazing there's
nothing like it in the late '7s or even
later.
>> Okay. So, so if I'm thinking about the
kind of push back that this idea would
receive, like I would think that
somebody might say, well, look, there
there's one question about how human
beings in fact behave. But there's
another question about what we should
do, and these just aren't that closely
related. Uh why did Migli think that
they were closely related?
>> Yeah, that's a really good uh point. I
mean I actually have a colleague um
who's a moral philosopher and a friend
and he just thinks our animality is
completely irrelevant to moral
philosophy but Mary Miji has a
particular understanding of what humans
are like and all animals in fact um we
are creatures that have certain kind of
motivational tendencies and those
motivational tendencies are a bit like
vectors. So you you might think of them
in terms of like the motivation to seek
attachment or to dominate or to play or
to create or to ask questions for
example. And for her human nature is
comprised of these motivational
tendencies. They actually form a knot
for her. And it's in the context of this
idea that she thinks you can begin to do
moral philosophy because for her if I
can go on a tiny bit um [clears throat]
>> for her what we value is connected to
what we need as animals. And then we're
motivated to go after what we need. So
you start with our our animality, then
you get an explanation as it were of
what we value because we value what we
need. And then we go after we're
motivated to go after what we need. So
the moral philosophy comes out then of
an account of human nature which is an
account of us as h as having this sort
of motivational kind of structural knot
at the heart of our being. Well, I can
see how the how science gives us uh a a
better characterization of what we're
actually like, right? We're animals just
like any other kind of animal. Uh our,
you know, our physical being gives us
certain needs and motivations. I can I
can totally see that, but I don't
understand how you get from there to
morality because I could say, well, you
know, my animal nature uh drives me to
really like u chocolate biscuits. I
think Mitchi would have approved of that
example. Um, but
>> yeah,
>> you know, maybe those chocolate biscuits
aren't that good for me and I need
something else over and above my, you
know, my my current motivations. And of
course, there are worse examples, right?
Maybe my maybe part of, you know, part
of my animal being drives me to satisfy
my desires at the expense of somebody
else or something like that. So don't we
need something else over and above this
scientific description of the kind of
creatures that we are in order to get us
morality?
>> Sure. Well, the idea is not just like we
are animals in some kind of vacuum,
right? We are social animals and we are
culture creating animals. So we in fact
develop uh cultural institutions and
practices like religious practices that
help us kind of keep our motivational
set in the in a kind of order or system
that serves us well. Okay. So for
example, we might have what some
philosophers sometimes call the stopping
modals like thou shalt not, you ought
not. Uh so we develop these systems that
kind of help us shape our ways of going
on in ways that you know in in in
beneficial ways. Um so it's not just
that we've got a kind of specimen type
biological creature um that does have
these kind of innate tendencies. Uh we
have to think of ourselves as these cre
uh culture building animals. Um and with
that comes in the kind of you know
constraints and uh and morality. In fact
>> Clara you mentioned our religious
tendencies and Migi had quite a lot to
say about those like what was her take
on religion?
>> So going back to these motivation these
motivational tendencies we have a need a
spiritual need to understand actually
and philosophy is is part of that. Okay.
So, the title of the book I wrote with
Rachel, metaphysical animals, we are we
have this uh we're motivated to to seek
comprehension and in a way some kind of
unity to our experience or at least
intelligibility. And religion is part of
that in fact. Um so, religion satisfies
that need. It also can not not to give a
reductive account of religion but or a
functional account but it it can also
satisfy or at least serve our needs for
communal
experience. Um it also can ritualize
certain kind of practices where we need
to uh express remorse or seek
forgiveness depending you know uh but so
depending on what what is needed the the
idea is that for her religion is a is a
natural human produced kind of
phenomenon um that serves our needs but
she she doesn't have a red she has great
respect for for religion I mean religion
is a the great world religions are like
a kind of storehouse of of language and
like rich moral terms that we can use to
make intelligible uh our experiences. So
she's she's agnostic in fact herself I
would say and her father was um was a
vicer and very influential figure on her
in fact
>> you're listening to philosophy talk
today we're asking about the life and
thought of Mary Migley with Clare McCall
from Durham University. So Claire, you
were just talking about Miji's attitude
towards religion and metaphysics more
broadly. And this is one of the things I
love the most about Miji's thinking that
she, as you say, she thinks we're
metaphysical animals. She doesn't think
that what philosophers do is this weird
sort of frivolous decadent thing that
just, you know, gets invented when
people are are sort of bored um and they
need to, you know, set themselves logic
puzzles. She thinks we're actually all
all human beings have this I don't know
innate instinctive natural need right
desire but also even need to think about
what is real and what is good it's so
philosophy is sort of continuous with
what we do in everyday life and she has
that lovely image that philosophy is
like plumbing
>> can you say a little bit about that
brilliant metaphor that that philosophy
is like plumbing
>> yes so you're absolutely right
philosophy for her is a basic human need
and it's going on all all the time and
we're all philosophers. So there's you
know all of all of us are philosophizing
when we're deciding what to do but then
there's the professional philosophers as
well I guess.
>> Yeah the paid plumbers.
>> Yeah there's the paid plumbers. So the
question is you know what they what
should they be doing? So first of all
the thought that philosophy is a bit
like plumbing that requires a certain
view of concepts. Okay. So, she doesn't
think a concept is a bit like a massive
sie that goes around and kind of scoops
up everything that looks vaguely sort of
similar and might fall under the same.
It's not like when she's scooping up red
things, she's scooping up uh like London
buses and apples and so on. She thinks
no, concepts have a structure and it's
not a simple structure. Concepts are
very very complex and our conceptual
systems are complex. So, with the
plumbing analogy, she's trying to say,
look, think about plumbing. Think about
a building. You know, I'm in Durham.
It's a medieval city. The plumbing that
was probably put in underneath the
cathedral is no doubt very faulty, but
it's been repaired and added to over the
years. It's now an absolute hodgepodge
of additions, some of them working and
some of them not working. Our concepts
are like that. There are these
structures, you know, that were created
at certain kind of periods of history
and have all of these added bits and
bits that don't kind of work. And what
the philosopher needs to do is sometimes
our our concepts lead to stagnation in
thought like there can be a blockage in
our thought or there can be a leak and
the philosopher needs to go in see where
there's the blockage using the skills of
analysis.
>> Yeah. I want to ask about analysis
because Mitchley's methodology seems
really at odds with what a lot of the
philosophers like around her and around
me are doing where uh you've got a
concept and to to understand what the
concept is you give sort of necessary
and sufficient conditions for falling
under that concept like what is it to
count as red so I take it that like th
this doesn't take into account a bunch
of the structure is there a
methodological upshot to thinking of
concepts does these uh structured old
legacy things.
>> Yeah, there is a methodological upshot.
So she's a kind of Vickensteinian I
guess. So Vickenstein famed he said look
don't think and what you need to do as a
philosopher is if if you're kind of
doing it in the midi mode is you need to
kind of look at where there are if you
like conceptual dysfunctions or where
there is blockages in thought in at the
historical moment that you're in where
you when you find yourself and we can
think of lots now right where we have
problems with our concepts we've got
troublesome concepts we you know we've
got whole sades of people who can't make
their experience intelligible in various
ways because of you know we've got
concepts that actually need more
structure. Uh so so the method is is not
to try and create a list of necessary
sufficient conditions sort of on your
own in your room in the armchair. You
need to look at the world and see where
there are actually conceptual problems
where there are blockages and then you
you need to obviously be able to work
out what those are. So there you kind of
need a little bit the notion of analysis
is more like the the kind of forensic
notion. You need to kind of dig deep and
see where the blockage is.
>> Which which gets us back to plumbing,
right? I mean that the that in a sense,
you know, all of us have plumbing. We
all use plumbing. We need plumbing. It's
very important. Uh but a lot of the time
we don't even
>> think about it. We may not even realize
